qtype: percentage of a quantity, rates, currency conversion, rounding
tex:
Dhanu has a model railway.
\newline

(a) He has a train that consists of a locomotive and 4 coaches.
\newline
The mass of the locomotive is 87 g and the mass of each coach is 52 g.
\newline

(i) (b) Work out the total mass of the train.
\newline

(c) Work out the mass of the locomotive as a percentage of the total mass of the train.
\newline

(d) The train is 61 cm long and travels at a speed of 18 cm/s.
\newline
It takes 4 seconds for the whole of the train to cross a bridge.
\newline
Calculate the length of the bridge.
\newline

(e) A new locomotive costs \$64.
\newline
Calculate the cost of the locomotive in rupees when the exchange rate is 1 rupee = \$0.0154 .
\newline
Give your answer correct to the nearest 10 rupees.
\newline

(f) The cost of a railway magazine increases by 12.5\% to \$2.70 .
\newline
Calculate the cost of the magazine before this increase.
\newline

\$ 

(g) Dhanu plays with his model railway from 06 50 to 11 15.
\newline
He then rides his bicycle for 3 hours.
\newline
Find the ratio time playing with model railway : time riding bicycle.
\newline
Give your answer in its simplest form.
\newline

 : 

(h) The value of Dhanu's model railway is \$550.
\newline
This value increases exponentially at a rate of r \% per year.
\newline
At the end of 5 years the value will be \$736.
\newline
Calculate the value of r.
\newline

r =

_rawBody:
Dhanu has a model railway.

\begin{parts}
\item He has a train that consists of a locomotive and 4 coaches.
The mass of the locomotive is 87 g and the mass of each coach is 52 g.

\begin{subparts}
\item Work out the total mass of the train.

\AnswerLine[][]
\Marks{2}

\item Work out the mass of the locomotive as a percentage of the total mass of the train.

\AnswerLine[][\%]
\Marks{1}
\end{subparts}

\item The train is 61 cm long and travels at a speed of 18 cm/s.
It takes 4 seconds for the whole of the train to cross a bridge.
Calculate the length of the bridge.

\AnswerLine[][cm]
\Marks{2}

\item A new locomotive costs \$64.
Calculate the cost of the locomotive in rupees when the exchange rate is 1 rupee = \$0.0154 .
Give your answer correct to the nearest 10 rupees.

\AnswerLine[][rupees]
\Marks{2}

\item The cost of a railway magazine increases by 12.5\% to \$2.70 .
Calculate the cost of the magazine before this increase.

\$ \AnswerLine[]
\Marks{2}

\item Dhanu plays with his model railway from 06 50 to 11 15.
He then rides his bicycle for 3 hours.
Find the ratio time playing with model railway : time riding bicycle.
Give your answer in its simplest form.

\AnswerLine[] : \AnswerLine[]
\Marks{3}

\item The value of Dhanu's model railway is \$550.
This value increases exponentially at a rate of r \% per year.
At the end of 5 years the value will be \$736.
Calculate the value of r.

r = \AnswerLine[]
\Marks{3}
\end{parts}

tex:
(a) Manjeet uses 220 litres of water each day.
\newline
She reduces the amount of water she uses by 15\%.
\newline
Calculate the number of litres of water she now uses each day.
\newline

(b) Manjeet has two mathematically similar bottles in her bathroom.
\newline
The large bottle holds 1.35 litres and is 29.7 cm high.
\newline
The small bottle holds 0.4 litres.
\newline
Calculate the height of the small bottle.
\newline

(c) Water from Manjeet's shower flows at a rate of 12 litres per minute.
\newline
The water from the shower flows into a tank that is a cuboid of length 90 cm and width 75 cm.
\newline
Calculate the increase in the level of water in the tank when the shower is used for 7 minutes.
\newline

content.content:
Manjeet uses 220 litres of water each day.
\newline
She reduces the amount of water she uses by 15\%.
\newline
Calculate the number of litres of water she now uses each day.
\newline

Manjeet has two mathematically similar bottles in her bathroom.
\newline
The large bottle holds 1.35 litres and is 29.7 cm high.
\newline
The small bottle holds 0.4 litres.
\newline
Calculate the height of the small bottle.
\newline

Water from Manjeet's shower flows at a rate of 12 litres per minute.
\newline
The water from the shower flows into a tank that is a cuboid of length 90 cm and width 75 cm.
\newline
Calculate the increase in the level of water in the tank when the shower is used for 7 minutes.
\newline

_rawBody:
\begin{parts}
\item Manjeet uses 220 litres of water each day.
She reduces the amount of water she uses by 15\%.
Calculate the number of litres of water she now uses each day.

\AnswerLine[][litres]
\Marks{2}

\item Manjeet has two mathematically similar bottles in her bathroom.
The large bottle holds 1.35 litres and is 29.7 cm high.
The small bottle holds 0.4 litres.
Calculate the height of the small bottle.

\AnswerLine[][cm]
\Marks{3}

\item Water from Manjeet's shower flows at a rate of 12 litres per minute.
The water from the shower flows into a tank that is a cuboid of length 90 cm and width 75 cm.
Calculate the increase in the level of water in the tank when the shower is used for 7 minutes.

\AnswerLine[][cm]
\Marks{3}
\end{parts}

tex:
A solid metal cone has radius 1.65 cm and slant height 4.70 cm.
\newline

(a) Calculate the total surface area of the cone.
\newline
\\
(The curved surface area, A, of a cone with radius r and slant height l is A = $\pi rl$.
\newline
)

(b) Find the angle the slant height makes with the base of the cone.
\newline

(c) (i) (d) Calculate the volume of the cone.
\newline
\\
(The volume, V, of a cone with radius r and height h is V = $\frac{1}{3}\pi r^{2}h$.
\newline
)

(e) A metal sphere with radius 5 cm is melted down to make cones identical to this one.
\newline
Calculate the number of complete identical cones that are made.
\newline
\\
(The volume, V, of a sphere with radius r is V = $\frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}$.
\newline
)

tex:
Suleika has six cards numbered 1 to 6.
\newline

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 & 6 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

(a) She takes one card at random, records the number and replaces the card.
\newline

(i) (b) Write down the probability that the number is 5 or 6.
\newline

(c) Suleika does this 300 times.
\newline
Find how many times she expects the number 5 or 6.
\newline

(d) Suleika takes two cards at random, without replacement.
\newline

(i) (e) Find the probability that the sum of the numbers on the two cards is 5.
\newline

(f) Find the probability that at least one of the numbers on the cards is a square number.
\newline

content.content:
She takes one card at random, records the number and replaces the card.
\newline

Write down the probability that the number is 5 or 6.
\newline

Suleika does this 300 times.
\newline
Find how many times she expects the number 5 or 6.
\newline

Suleika takes two cards at random, without replacement.
\newline

Find the probability that the sum of the numbers on the two cards is 5.
\newline

Find the probability that at least one of the numbers on the cards is a square number.
\newline

stem.content:
Suleika has six cards numbered 1 to 6.
\newline

qtype: Venn diagrams, set notation, intersection, complement
tex:
This year, 40 students have each travelled by one or more of plane (P), train (T) or boat (B).
\newline
\\
7 have travelled only by plane.
\newline
\\
11 have travelled only by train.
\newline
\\
9 have travelled only by boat.
\newline
\\
n(P $\cup$ T) = 8\\
n(B $\cup$ T) = 3\\
n(B $\cup$ P) = 6

(a) Complete the Venn diagram.
\newline

(b) Find n(P $\cap$ B).
\newline

(c) Use set notation to complete the statement.
\newline
\\
(P $\cap$ T $\cap$ B)$^{\prime}$ = 

(d) Two students are chosen at random.
\newline
Calculate the probability that they both have travelled only by plane.
\newline

(e) Two students are chosen at random from those who have travelled by train.
\newline
Calculate the probability that they both have also travelled by plane.
\newline

content.content:
Complete the Venn diagram.
\newline

Find n(P $\cap$ B).
\newline

Use set notation to complete the statement.
\newline
\\
(P $\cap$ T $\cap$ B)$^{\prime}$ = ...........................

Two students are chosen at random.
\newline
Calculate the probability that they both have travelled only by plane.
\newline

Two students are chosen at random from those who have travelled by train.
\newline
Calculate the probability that they both have also travelled by plane.
\newline

\newline
\\

\\

\newline
\\
9 have travelled only by boat.
\newline
\\
n(P $\cup$ T) = 8\\
n(B $\cup$ T) = 3\\
n(B $\cup$ P) = 6
